Stat218Applet 0.0.0.9000
  • Reference

2-Sample Proportions Confidence Interval

Source: R/ci_2prop.R
ci_2prop.Rd

Constructs a confidence interval for the difference between two population proportions (\(\pi_1 - \pi_2\)) using one of three methods: the 2SD simulation method, a general simulation method, or a theory-based approach.

This function accepts input in two ways:

  • Summary Statistics: You already know the number of successes and sample sizes for both groups. Pass success_1, n_1, success_2, and n_2 directly. Note that method = "2SD" and method = "simulation" are not available with summary statistics.

  • Raw Data: You have an actual dataset loaded into R. Pass formula, data, and success_level instead, and the function counts successes for you automatically. All three methods are available with raw data.

ci_2prop(
  success_1 = NULL,
  n_1 = NULL,
  success_2 = NULL,
  n_2 = NULL,
  group_names = NULL,
  formula = NULL,
  data = NULL,
  success_level = NULL,
  conf_level = 0.95,
  method = "2SD",
  sim_reps = 1000
)

Arguments

success_1

A whole number representing the count of successes in Group 1. For example, if 45 out of 100 patients recovered, success_1 = 45. Only used when NOT providing formula and data.

n_1

The sample size of Group 1. Only used when NOT providing formula and data.

success_2

A whole number representing the count of successes in Group 2. Only used when NOT providing formula and data.

n_2

The sample size of Group 2. Only used when NOT providing formula and data.

group_names

A character vector of length 2 giving meaningful names to the two groups. For example, group_names = c("Treatment", "Control"). Defaults to c("Group 1", "Group 2"). When using raw data, group names are extracted automatically from the grouping variable. Only used when NOT providing formula and data.

formula

A two-sided formula of the form Response ~ Group identifying the categorical response variable and the grouping variable in your dataset. For example, formula = Recovered ~ Treatment. Only used when NOT providing summary statistics.

data

A data frame (loaded from a .csv, .txt, or .xlsx file) containing the variables named in formula. Only used when NOT providing summary statistics.

success_level

A character string specifying which value of the response variable counts as a "success". For example, if the response variable contains "Yes" and "No", use success_level = "Yes". Required when using formula and data.

conf_level

The desired confidence level as a decimal. Default is 0.95 for a 95% confidence interval. Common choices are 0.90, 0.95, and 0.99. Note that method = "2SD" only works at 0.95.

method

The method used to construct the confidence interval. Must be one of:

"2SD"

(default) The 2SD Simulation Method. An independent bootstrap is generated by resampling with replacement from each group separately.The interval is \((\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2) \pm 2 \times SD\). Only valid for 95% confidence interval limit. If you need a different confidence level, use method = "simulation".

"simulation"

The Bootstrap Simulation Method. Same independent bootstrap as 2SD, but uses the middle conf_level% of the simulated distribution to find the bounds. Works for any confidence level.

"theory"

The Theory-Based Method. Uses the unpooled standard error formula and the appropriate Z* multiplier. Works for any confidence level and with both summary statistics and raw data.

sim_reps

The number of bootstrap samples when method = "2SD" or method = "simulation". Default is 1000. Increasing to 5000 gives a more stable interval.

Value

An S3 object of class stat218_2prop_ci containing all computed values. You can use the following methods on the result:

print(result)

Displays a clean summary of the interval including both group proportions, the observed difference, SD of sampling distribution, and the interval bounds.

plot(result)

Shows the bootstrap or theoretical distribution with the confidence interval shaded and labeled, a forest plot of the interval, and a beautified 2x2 contingency table.

plot_steps(result)

Displays a detailed three-panel step-by-step construction of the interval with proper fraction notation.

Details

The Core Idea

A two-sample proportion confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for the true difference \(\pi_1 - \pi_2\). A positive interval suggests Group 1 has a higher proportion; a negative interval suggests Group 2 is higher; an interval containing zero is consistent with no difference between the two groups.

The 2SD Method

An independent bootstrap where successes and failures from each group are resampled with replacement separately. The difference in simulated proportions is recorded for each repetition. The SD of those differences estimates the SE, and the interval is point estimate \(\pm 2 \times SD\). Note that unlike test_2prop which uses a permutation approach, the CI bootstrap resamples each group independently — this is correct for confidence intervals where we are not assuming the null hypothesis.

Theory Method

Uses the unpooled standard error (unlike the hypothesis test which uses a pooled proportion). This is because for a confidence interval we are not assuming the two proportions are equal: $$SE = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}_1(1-\hat{p}_1)}{n_1} + \frac{\hat{p}_2(1-\hat{p}_2)}{n_2}}$$

Validity Conditions for Theory Method

All four cells in the 2x2 contingency table must have at least 10 observations:

  • Successes in Group 1 \(\geq 10\)

  • Failures in Group 1 \(\geq 10\)

  • Successes in Group 2 \(\geq 10\)

  • Failures in Group 2 \(\geq 10\)

Examples

# --- Summary Statistics (theory, default) ---
result <- ci_2prop(
  success_1 = 45, n_1 = 100,
  success_2 = 25, n_2 = 100,
  group_names = c("Treatment", "Control"),
  method = "theory"
)
print(result)
#> 
#> ── 95% Confidence Interval for Difference in Proportions (Theory) ──────────────
#> ℹ Treatment: p-hat1 = 0.45 (successes = 45, n = 100)
#> ℹ Control: p-hat2 = 0.25 (successes = 25, n = 100)
#> ℹ Point Estimate (p-hat1 - p-hat2): 0.2
#> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.066
#> • Interval: (0.0707, 0.3293)
plot(result)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>
plot_steps(result)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>

# --- Summary Statistics (theory, 90% CI) ---
result2 <- ci_2prop(
  success_1 = 45, n_1 = 100,
  success_2 = 25, n_2 = 100,
  group_names = c("Treatment", "Control"),
  conf_level = 0.90, method = "theory"
)
print(result2)
#> 
#> ── 90% Confidence Interval for Difference in Proportions (Theory) ──────────────
#> ℹ Treatment: p-hat1 = 0.45 (successes = 45, n = 100)
#> ℹ Control: p-hat2 = 0.25 (successes = 25, n = 100)
#> ℹ Point Estimate (p-hat1 - p-hat2): 0.2
#> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.066
#> • Interval: (0.0915, 0.3085)
plot(result2)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>
plot_steps(result2)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>

# --- Raw Data (2SD method) ---
car_data <- mtcars
car_data$transmission <- ifelse(mtcars$am == 1, "Manual", "Automatic")
car_data$engine <- ifelse(mtcars$vs == 1, "Straight", "V-shaped")
result3 <- ci_2prop(
  formula = transmission ~ engine, data = car_data,
  success_level = "Manual", method = "2SD"
)
#> ✔ Data extracted from "transmission" ~ "engine":
#> • V-shaped: 6 successes out of 18
#> • Straight: 7 successes out of 14
#> ℹ Success level: "Manual"
print(result3)
#> 
#> ── 95% Confidence Interval for Difference in Proportions (2sd) ─────────────────
#> ℹ V-shaped: p-hat1 = 0.3333 (successes = 6, n = 18)
#> ℹ Straight: p-hat2 = 0.5 (successes = 7, n = 14)
#> ℹ Point Estimate (p-hat1 - p-hat2): -0.1667
#> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.1717
#> • Interval: (-0.5101, 0.1768)
plot(result3)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>
plot_steps(result3)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>

# --- Raw Data (simulation, 90% CI) ---
result4 <- ci_2prop(
  formula = transmission ~ engine, data = car_data,
  success_level = "Manual", conf_level = 0.90,
  method = "simulation"
)
#> ✔ Data extracted from "transmission" ~ "engine":
#> • V-shaped: 6 successes out of 18
#> • Straight: 7 successes out of 14
#> ℹ Success level: "Manual"
print(result4)
#> 
#> ── 90% Confidence Interval for Difference in Proportions (Simulation) ──────────
#> ℹ V-shaped: p-hat1 = 0.3333 (successes = 6, n = 18)
#> ℹ Straight: p-hat2 = 0.5 (successes = 7, n = 14)
#> ℹ Point Estimate (p-hat1 - p-hat2): -0.1667
#> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.1653
#> • Interval: (-0.4365, 0.119)
plot(result4)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>
plot_steps(result4)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>

# --- Raw Data (theory) ---
result5 <- ci_2prop(
  formula = transmission ~ engine, data = car_data,
  success_level = "Manual", method = "theory"
)
#> ✔ Data extracted from "transmission" ~ "engine":
#> • V-shaped: 6 successes out of 18
#> • Straight: 7 successes out of 14
#> ℹ Success level: "Manual"
#> Warning: ! Validity conditions for the theory-based interval may not be met.
#> ℹ All four cells in the 2x2 table must have at least 10 observations.
#> ℹ Minimum cell count found: 6.
#> ℹ Consider using `method = "2SD"` or `method = "simulation"` instead.
print(result5)
#> 
#> ── 95% Confidence Interval for Difference in Proportions (Theory) ──────────────
#> ℹ V-shaped: p-hat1 = 0.3333 (successes = 6, n = 18)
#> ℹ Straight: p-hat2 = 0.5 (successes = 7, n = 14)
#> ℹ Point Estimate (p-hat1 - p-hat2): -0.1667
#> ℹ SD of Sampling Distribution: 0.1738
#> • Interval: (-0.5073, 0.174)
#> Warning: ! Validity conditions may not be met — at least one cell has fewer than 10
#>   observations.
#> ℹ Minimum cell count: 6.
#> ℹ Consider using `method = "2SD"` or `method = "simulation"`.
plot(result5)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>
plot_steps(result5)
#> Error: Can't find method for generic `/(e1, e2)`:
#> - e1: <ggplot2::ggplot>
#> - e2: <ggplot2::ggplot>
